analysis: Across the top heuristics the pipeline is consistent: (1) fast Pareto/nondominated filtering, (2) cheap vectorized proxy scores (rectangular box volume ± scalarized weight probes) to form an O(k)-shortlist, (3) an exact greedy/CELF selection using cached singleton HVs (and selective pairwise caches), and (4) a bounded 1-for-1 local-swap cleanup. Stronger variants (Heuristics 1–5, 3) tune shortlist sizing, use better hybrid scores, and spend more exact HV work (or smarter greedy scans), yielding higher hypervolume at the cost of more HV calls. Weaker designs (20–22, 17–19) mainly differ by stricter HV-call budgets, smaller tight shortlists, more conservative caching/fallbacks, or by heavier Python-level loops; they therefore do fewer exact HV evaluations and lose quality. Notable alternatives: Heuristic 10 replaces exact HV with a many-weight HVC approximation (cheap, scalable), and Heuristic 6 (GSI_LS) is essentially brute‑force insertion + full local search and is HV‑expensive. Recurrent inefficiencies: repeated construction of pg.hypervolume objects inside inner loops, many Python-level "for cand in list" membership checks, recomputing (ref - points) and not reusing vectorized arrays, and lack of batched or incremental HV operations. Effective patterns to retain: precompute diffs and singleton HVs, use CELF lazy timestamps to minimize recomputations, selective pairwise caching when shortlist ≤ ~40, deterministic shortlist sizing proportional to k with sensible caps, and an explicit HV-call budget. Tradeoffs are clear: more exact HVs + larger shortlists → better solution; tighter budgets + aggressive pruning → faster but poorer HV.
experience: Keep the four-stage pipeline, but (a) vectorize everything possible (precompute ref−pts, rect volumes), (b) use sets/boolean masks not list membership for O(1) checks, (c) CELF+singleton cache+selective pairwise cache minimises HV calls, (d) use an adaptive O(k) shortlist (cap by Pareto size), and (e) expose an HV-call budget and an approximate HVC fallback (many-weight scalarization) to trade quality vs time deterministically.
current_reflection: Keep the four-stage pipeline, but (a) vectorize everything possible (precompute ref−pts, rect volumes), (b) use sets/boolean masks not list membership for O(1) checks, (c) CELF+singleton cache+selective pairwise cache minimises HV calls, (d) use an adaptive O(k) shortlist (cap by Pareto size), and (e) expose an HV-call budget and an approximate HVC fallback (many-weight scalarization) to trade quality vs time deterministically.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.

Design next heuristics by enforcing an O(k) shortlist, always cache singleton HVs, use CELF lazy evaluation with timestamps, apply cheap box-volume upper bounds to prune candidates, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and swap iterations, and prefer deterministic stable ordering. These focused changes yield most HV-quality gain per expensive HV call.

Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.
bad_reflections: Favor small, Pareto-aware shortlists and CELF lazy-greedy with cached singleton HVs. Use vectorized box/scalar proxies to rank candidates, selectively cache pairwise HVs only when list ≤ ~40, enforce an HV-call budget, and prune using cheap upper bounds. Preallocate buffers and minimize vstack/copy inside hot loops; prefer deterministic seeds and tie-breakers. These choices give best hypervolume per HV call.

Favor a hybrid score (box volume × multi‑weight scalarization), build an adaptive shortlist (k‑ and pareto‑aware), cache singleton HVs plus small pairwise tables, use CELF lazy greedy with box upper‑bound pruning, cap HV calls, then run a small bounded deterministic swap (incremental HV updates). Vectorize dominance/scoring, preallocate buffers, and avoid expensive Python list operations for membership to get better quality/runtime.

Design candidates by (1) Pareto prefilter + deterministic blended box+scalar shortlist sized O(k); (2) CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton HVs and small selective pairwise cache; (3) tight box/HV upper‑bound pruning and an HV‑call budget with box‑fallback estimates; (4) incremental or cached local‑swap evaluations; and (5) vectorized filters and set membership to cut Python overhead. Tune shortlist size and weight probes for the budget/instance.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: vectorize, Pareto prefilter, O(k) shortlist, CELF+, singleton cache, selective pairwise, box upper bounds, HV budget, multi-weight scalarization, deterministic
- Advice: Vectorize dominance and precompute ref‑points/rect volumes; use boolean masks/sets. Prefilter Pareto, build an adaptive O(k) shortlist (cap by Pareto) via box‑volume + few‑weight scalarization. Run CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton HVs, enable pairwise cache only for tiny shortlists (≤40), batch HV calls, use timestamps and incremental HVC deltas, enforce an HV‑call budget, seed tie‑breaks, and always return exactly k.
- Avoid: Nested HV recomputations, Python list membership scans, unbounded pairwise caching, heavy vstack/copy in hot loops, nondeterministic ordering, and no fallback when budget exhausted.
- Explanation: Cuts exact HV calls and Python overhead, gives deterministic quality/time tradeoffs via budgeted approximate HVC fallback, yielding consistent high‑quality k‑subsets.
